encounter.setting: School psychology — parent feedback session after psychoeducational evaluation
encounter.referral_context: 9-year-old girl evaluated for suspected learning disability. Parents were told she 'tested at the 50th percentile' but are confused because her teacher says she is 'clearly very bright.'

phases.dialogue: I'm confused. The previous psychologist said Emma scored at the 50th percentile on IQ — which sounds average. But her teacher says she's clearly above average. And Emma struggles with reading despite being good at math and science. How can she be average and also struggling?

chart_reveals.label: Parent concern
chart_reveals.value: Discrepancy between FSIQ (50th %ile) and teacher's perception of above-average ability; concurrent reading struggles

chart_reveals.label: Assessment history
chart_reveals.value: Prior WISC-V administered 6 months ago; parent never received complete interpretation session

phases.dialogue: The report shows something called 'VCI' at the 84th percentile, 'VSI' at the 91st percentile, and 'PSI' at the 16th percentile. The Full Scale IQ was 100. Those seem very different to me — how can all these scores average to 100?

chart_reveals.label: WISC-V Verbal Comprehension (VCI)
chart_reveals.value: 114 (84th %ile) — High Average; strong language and verbal reasoning

chart_reveals.label: WISC-V Visual Spatial (VSI)
chart_reveals.value: 118 (88th %ile) — High Average; strong nonverbal and spatial reasoning

chart_reveals.label: WISC-V Processing Speed (PSI)
chart_reveals.value: 82 (12th %ile) — Low; significant relative weakness

chart_reveals.label: WISC-V Full Scale IQ (FSIQ)
chart_reveals.value: 100 (50th %ile) — but high index score variability limits interpretive validity of FSIQ

phases.clinician_prompt: Let me show you what the different scores mean — the Full Scale IQ doesn't tell the whole story.

phases.dialogue: So she's actually strong in language and visual reasoning, but something about her processing speed is low? Could that be why she reads slowly? She's not slow in thinking — she just takes forever to get her thoughts on paper or to read a full page.

chart_reveals.label: Academic observation
chart_reveals.value: Slow written output and reading rate despite strong verbal and reasoning ability — consistent with PSI deficit

chart_reveals.label: WIAT-4 Reading Rate
chart_reveals.value: 75 (5th %ile) — significantly below FSIQ and VCI; consistent with processing speed weakness

chart_reveals.label: Formulation
chart_reveals.value: Profile consistent with processing speed weakness as primary deficit underlying reading rate difficulty

phases.clinician_prompt: Exactly — that's a very insightful connection. Let me explain why the Full Scale IQ number alone was misleading.

questions.prompt: Emma's WISC-V shows index scores ranging from 82 to 118. What is the correct interpretation of her Full Scale IQ of 100, given this variability?

options.A: The FSIQ of 100 is valid because it is the most reliable composite score and should guide all diagnostic and placement decisions
options.B: The FSIQ has limited interpretive validity due to extreme index score variability — the index profile (VCI, VSI, PSI) provides more clinically useful information
options.C: The FSIQ confirms average ability — the index variability is normal and expected in all children
options.D: The FSIQ should be discarded and the highest index score (VSI=118) should be used as the true ability estimate

questions.explanation: When significant intra-individual variability exists across WISC-V index scores (typically a 1.5 SD / 23-point range is clinically significant), the Full Scale IQ becomes a misleading summary statistic — it averages very different abilities into a single number that does not represent any one cognitive domain accurately. WISC-V interpretation guidelines recommend reporting and interpreting index scores as the primary level of analysis when variability is high, because each index measures a distinct cognitive ability. In Emma's case, the VCI (114) and VSI (118) reflect genuine high-average ability, while the PSI (82) reflects a specific processing deficit — FSIQ of 100 obscures this important diagnostic information.

distractor_rationale.A: FSIQ reliability is not the issue — the issue is construct validity. A reliable average of unequal abilities is still a misleading representation of cognitive functioning when abilities are discrepant.
distractor_rationale.C: A 36-point range (82 to 118) across index scores represents clinically significant variability. Research shows this level of scatter is uncommon in the normative population and warrants index-level interpretation.
distractor_rationale.D: Selecting the highest score as 'true ability' is not a standardized interpretive approach. The PSW model and other frameworks use the full profile — not a cherry-picked peak.

questions.prompt: Which assessment-based classification best fits Emma's profile: strong VCI/VSI, weak PSI, slow reading rate?

options.A: Intellectual Disability — FSIQ of 100 is not consistent with this, but the processing speed deficit indicates global limitation
options.B: Gifted with Twice Exceptionality (2e) — high cognitive ability coexisting with a specific learning disability in reading
options.C: ADHD, Predominantly Inattentive — processing speed deficits always indicate attention problems
options.D: Developmental Coordination Disorder — processing speed deficits reflect motor coordination problems

questions.explanation: Twice exceptionality (2e) describes students with high intellectual ability in some domains who simultaneously demonstrate a specific learning disability or developmental condition. Emma's profile shows genuine cognitive strengths (VCI=114, VSI=118 — above average language and spatial reasoning) alongside a specific processing speed deficit (PSI=82) that undermines reading rate despite strong cognitive resources. This is a classic 2e presentation. The 'average' FSIQ masked her strengths, which explains why the teacher's observations ('clearly bright') and the test results ('50th percentile') seemed contradictory.

distractor_rationale.A: Intellectual Disability requires FSIQ ≤70 across multiple cognitive domains, adaptive behavior deficits, and onset in the developmental period. Emma's profile shows strong abilities in multiple areas.
distractor_rationale.C: Processing speed deficits are associated with ADHD but are not pathognomonic — they occur in SLD, TBI, anxiety, and 2e profiles. A full attention evaluation would be needed before concluding ADHD.
distractor_rationale.D: Developmental Coordination Disorder involves motor skills — the PSI deficit could have a motor component (written output), but DCD requires a thorough motor evaluation and does not explain the reading rate deficit.

My wife says I'm irritable and I fly off the handle. The ER said the CT was negative. My boss thinks I'm malingering.

chart_reveals.value: Rear-end MVA; head contact with headrest; no windshield or airbag impact

chart_reveals.label: Acute presentation
chart_reveals.value: Brief confusion/foggy period (< 1 min); no confirmed LOC; GCS 15 at scene

chart_reveals.label: Post-acute course
chart_reveals.value: Symptom onset day 1 post-injury; 8 weeks persistent — meets criteria for Persistent Post-Concussion Symptoms

chart_reveals.label: Pre-morbid
chart_reveals.value: Attorney; high premorbid functioning; no prior head injury or neuropsychiatric history

phases.clinician_prompt: Walk me through what you remember from right before impact to the next morning.

phases.dialogue: I understand I need to take some memory tests. I want to be clear — I'm not exaggerating. I have everything to gain by going back to work. I have active cases, clients depending on me. I just physically cannot do what I need to do right now.
behavioral_tags: secondary gain concern raised by referral source

chart_reveals.label: Motivation concerns
chart_reveals.value: Referral note mentions 'possible malingering'; patient explicitly denies; has active litigant status

chart_reveals.label: Behavioral observations
chart_reveals.value: Appropriate frustration; no inconsistency between stated and observed symptoms

chart_reveals.label: SVT plan
chart_reveals.value: Embedded and standalone symptom validity tests (e.g., TOMM, MSVT) included in battery as standard protocol

phases.clinician_prompt: I want to explain that symptom validity testing is a routine part of any neuropsychological evaluation — it actually protects you as a patient.

questions.prompt: Why is a normal CT scan at time of injury NOT sufficient to rule out mild TBI and its cognitive sequelae?

options.A: CT scans cannot detect injury if administered within 24 hours of the accident
options.B: CT scans detect gross structural pathology (hemorrhage, fracture) but cannot detect diffuse axonal injury, microhemorrhages, or neurometabolic changes that underlie mTBI symptoms
options.C: The CT scan may have been incorrectly read — a repeat CT with contrast would be definitive
options.D: Normal CT scans confirm that symptoms are functional (psychogenic) rather than neurological

questions.explanation: mTBI pathophysiology primarily involves diffuse axonal injury (DAI) — stretching and shearing of axonal connections from rotational/acceleration-deceleration forces — and neurometabolic dysfunction (ionic flux, glutamate excitotoxicity, mitochondrial dysfunction). CT scans detect gross structural lesions (hemorrhage, contusion, skull fracture) with high sensitivity but are insensitive to DAI and neurometabolic changes. MRI (particularly susceptibility-weighted imaging, SWI, and diffusion tensor imaging, DTI) is more sensitive, but many mTBI cases have normal structural MRI despite real pathophysiology measurable on functional imaging and neuropsychological testing.

distractor_rationale.A: CT scan sensitivity is not meaningfully affected by time-of-administration in the acute window. Timing is not the reason for limited sensitivity to mTBI.
distractor_rationale.C: CT with contrast enhances vascular structures — it does not improve detection of diffuse axonal injury or neurometabolic changes. Contrast CT is not the appropriate next imaging step for mTBI.
distractor_rationale.D: A normal CT does not establish a functional (psychogenic) etiology. This conclusion is not neurologically supported and risks dismissing real neurophysiological injury.

questions.prompt: The referral note suggests malingering. What is the neuropsychologist's most appropriate approach to symptom validity in this evaluation?

options.A: Skip formal SVT because the patient denies malingering and appears credible
options.B: Include embedded and standalone symptom validity tests as standard protocol; interpret performance in context of the full clinical picture
options.C: Use only the MMPI-2 validity scales as the primary measure of symptom credibility
options.D: Assume malingering given the active litigation status and recommend denial of disability claim

questions.explanation: Symptom validity testing (SVT) is a professional and ethical standard in neuropsychological evaluations — not a test used only when malingering is suspected. Standalone SVTs (e.g., TOMM, MSVT, WMT) and embedded performance validity indicators (PVTs within standard tests) should be administered routinely in all forensic and high-stakes clinical evaluations. The neuropsychologist then interprets SVT performance in context: below-chance performance suggests non-credible effort; results within normal limits provide evidentiary support for the credibility of other cognitive findings. Active litigation status alone does not establish malingering — base rates of malingering vary widely and require convergent evidence.

distractor_rationale.A: Clinician impression of credibility is insufficient to replace objective SVT. Credible-appearing patients occasionally show non-credible performance; skeptical-appearing patients often show valid effort. SVT is a scientific safeguard, not a character judgment.
distractor_rationale.C: MMPI-2 validity scales assess self-report personality and symptom endorsement — they are not designed as primary neuropsychological performance validity measures. MMPI-2 can complement SVT but does not substitute for cognitive performance validity testing.
distractor_rationale.D: Assuming malingering based on litigation status is ethically impermissible (APA Standards 9.01, 9.06) and scientifically indefensible. Litigation does not significantly increase base rates of malingering beyond what SVTs are designed to detect.

encounter.referral_context: Self-referred 34-year-old female presenting with concentration difficulties and emotional dysregulation following a recent job loss. Primary care physician noted possible ADHD and recommended psychological evaluation.

phases.dialogue: My doctor said I should come get tested for ADHD. Honestly, I've always been kind of scattered, but it's gotten so much worse since I lost my job two months ago. I can't focus on anything — like, I'll sit down to work on my resume and three hours later I've done nothing. I just stare at the screen or end up scrolling on my phone. It's really frustrating.

chart_reveals.label: Presenting concern
chart_reveals.value: Concentration difficulties; possible ADHD evaluation requested by PCP

chart_reveals.label: Onset/precipitant
chart_reveals.value: Symptoms worsened significantly 2 months ago after involuntary job loss

phases.clinician_prompt: Tell me what brings you in today.

phases.dialogue: In school I was always a good student — honor roll, got into a decent college. I mean, sure, I'd procrastinate sometimes, but who doesn't? I don't think I ever had problems like this before. But lately... I don't know, I just feel like my brain is full of static. I'm not sleeping well either. I wake up at like 3 or 4 a.m. and just lie there thinking about money, about what went wrong. Sometimes I feel like such a failure. I used to love cooking, going out with friends — now I just can't be bothered. Everything feels like too much effort.

chart_reveals.label: Academic history
chart_reveals.value: No childhood attention or behavioral concerns; honor roll student through college

chart_reveals.label: Sleep disturbance

chart_reveals.label: Anhedonia

phases.clinician_prompt: Can you tell me more about what your focus and attention were like before this all started — say, back in school or at previous jobs?

phases.dialogue: Sorry, what was the question again? I keep zoning out. I'm not trying to be rude — it's just like my mind goes somewhere else. Honestly, I feel kind of foggy right now, like I'm moving through mud.

chart_reveals.label: Attention
chart_reveals.value: Difficulty sustaining attention during interview; required repetition of several questions

chart_reveals.label: Psychomotor activity
chart_reveals.value: Mild psychomotor retardation; slowed speech latency; reduced gestural expression

chart_reveals.label: Mood/Affect
chart_reveals.value: Self-reported mood: 'low and foggy'; affect constricted with sad quality

phases.clinician_prompt: I noticed you seemed to drift off for a moment. What's going through your mind right now?

phases.dialogue: My sister says I've been different since the layoff. She says I barely text back anymore and I sound 'flat' on the phone. She actually asked if I was depressed. My mom also mentioned that nobody in our family has ever had ADHD, but my grandmother had depression pretty bad.
behavioral_tags: eyes become glassy when mentioning sister's concern

chart_reveals.label: Family observation
chart_reveals.value: Sister reports marked withdrawal and flat affect since job loss; concerned about depression

chart_reveals.label: Family psychiatric history
chart_reveals.value: Maternal grandmother had major depressive disorder; no family history of ADHD

chart_reveals.label: PHQ-9 (completed in waiting room)
chart_reveals.value: Score: 19 (moderately severe depression)

phases.clinician_prompt: Have any family members or close friends commented on how you've been doing lately?

questions.prompt: Based on the full clinical picture — including developmental history, symptom onset, behavioral observations, collateral report, and PHQ-9 score — which of the following is the most likely primary diagnosis?

options.A: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder, Predominantly Inattentive Presentation
options.B: Major Depressive Disorder, Single Episode
options.C: Adjustment Disorder with Depressed Mood
options.D: Persistent Depressive Disorder (Dysthymia)

questions.explanation: The clinical presentation is most consistent with Major Depressive Disorder (MDD), single episode. The patient demonstrates depressed mood, anhedonia, early morning awakening, psychomotor retardation, concentration difficulties, and feelings of worthlessness — all emerging within a discrete 2-month period following a stressor. Crucially, there is no childhood history of attentional difficulties, the PHQ-9 score is 19 (moderately severe), and family history is positive for depression. The concentration problems are best understood as a cognitive symptom of depression ('pseudodementia') rather than a primary attentional disorder.

distractor_rationale.A: ADHD requires symptom onset before age 12 and evidence of chronic inattention across settings. This patient had no childhood attention problems and was an honor-roll student; her concentration difficulties began abruptly 2 months ago with other depressive symptoms.
distractor_rationale.C: Adjustment Disorder with Depressed Mood could be considered given the clear stressor, but the severity and number of symptoms (meeting full MDD criteria including psychomotor retardation, anhedonia, sleep disturbance, and a PHQ-9 of 19) exceed what is typical for an adjustment reaction.
distractor_rationale.D: Persistent Depressive Disorder requires symptoms lasting at least 2 years. This patient's symptom duration is only 2 months, ruling out dysthymia.

questions.prompt: During the clinical interview, the patient's difficulty sustaining attention and reported 'brain fog' raised the question of ADHD. Which of the following DSM-5-TR criteria for ADHD is most clearly NOT met in this case?

options.A: Several inattentive or hyperactive-impulsive symptoms were present prior to age 12
options.B: The symptoms are present in two or more settings
options.C: There is clear evidence that the symptoms interfere with functioning
options.D: The symptoms do not occur exclusively during the course of a psychotic disorder

questions.explanation: DSM-5-TR requires that several inattentive or hyperactive-impulsive symptoms were present prior to age 12 years. This patient explicitly denies childhood attention problems and was academically successful throughout school, with concentration difficulties emerging only at age 34 following a depressive episode. This criterion is clearly not met.

distractor_rationale.B: The patient does report concentration difficulties across settings (job applications at home, general daily tasks), so a cross-setting criterion could arguably be met if ADHD were present.
distractor_rationale.C: The patient reports clear functional impairment (inability to complete her resume, social withdrawal), so interference with functioning is evident regardless of the underlying cause.
distractor_rationale.D: There is no evidence of a psychotic disorder, so this exclusion criterion is met — it does not help differentiate against ADHD in this case.

But I hate writing and math. My hand gets really tired and the numbers get all jumbled up. My teacher says I'm smart but lazy, which isn't fair because I try really hard.

chart_reveals.label: Patient self-report

She notes he avoids math worksheets and sometimes puts his head down during independent work time.

questions.prompt: Given the referral question and behavioral observations, which combination of assessment instruments would be most appropriate for the core battery in this evaluation?

options.A: WISC-V, Beery VMI, WIAT-4 (Written Expression and Mathematics subtests), and CELF-5
options.B: WISC-V, Beery VMI, WIAT-4 (Written Expression and Mathematics subtests), and Grooved Pegboard
options.C: KABC-II, Conners-4, WIAT-4 (all subtests), and BASC-3
options.D: WISC-V, WIAT-4 (Reading and Written Expression subtests only), and BRIEF-2

questions.explanation: This battery is most appropriate because the WISC-V provides a comprehensive cognitive profile to identify the expected verbal-nonverbal discrepancy and examine processing speed and working memory. The Beery VMI directly assesses visual-motor integration relevant to the graphomotor concerns. The WIAT-4 Written Expression and Mathematics subtests target the two academic areas of concern. The Grooved Pegboard adds fine motor dexterity assessment given the prominent motor difficulties observed (immature pencil grasp, hand fatigue), which is critical for differentiating a specific learning disability from a motor coordination issue contributing to written output problems.

distractor_rationale.A: Including the CELF-5 (language assessment) is unnecessary given that the child has advanced verbal skills, early language milestones, and above-grade-level reading. Language is clearly a strength, not an area of concern. The Grooved Pegboard would be more clinically relevant given the motor observations.
distractor_rationale.C: The Conners-4 and BASC-3 are behavioral/attention rating scales. While ADHD could be a differential, the primary presentation is a clear verbal-performance split with fine motor difficulties, not an attentional concern. The KABC-II, while valid, does not provide the same processing speed/working memory index detail as the WISC-V for this referral question. This battery lacks direct motor assessment.
distractor_rationale.D: This battery omits Mathematics from the WIAT-4 despite math being a core area of concern, and excludes any motor assessment despite prominent graphomotor difficulties. The BRIEF-2 assesses executive function, which is not the primary referral question.

questions.prompt: Based on the developmental history, behavioral observations, and collateral information, which differential diagnostic consideration is most important to rule out before concluding this is solely a Specific Learning Disability?

options.A: Intellectual Developmental Disorder (Intellectual Disability)
options.B: Developmental Coordination Disorder
options.C: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder, Predominantly Inattentive Presentation
options.D: Autism Spectrum Disorder

questions.explanation: Developmental Coordination Disorder (DCD) is the most critical differential to rule out. The child demonstrates an immature pencil grasp, hand fatigue, slow graphomotor output, fine motor delays identified at age 7, and difficulty with spatial organization of numbers — all consistent with a motor coordination disorder. If DCD is present, the written expression and math difficulties may be substantially explained by motor deficits rather than (or in addition to) a specific learning disability in these areas. This distinction has direct implications for intervention planning.

distractor_rationale.A: Intellectual Developmental Disorder is unlikely given the child's advanced vocabulary, early language development, above-grade-level reading, strong verbal engagement, and good performance on verbal cognitive tasks. The profile is highly uneven, not globally low.
distractor_rationale.C: While losing track of sequential steps could suggest inattention, the child demonstrates sustained attention on preferred tasks and the observation pattern is more consistent with working memory demands of multi-step math than a primary attention deficit. There is no report of pervasive inattention across non-demanding contexts.
distractor_rationale.D: Autism Spectrum Disorder is not well-supported. The child is socially engaged, emotionally expressive, conversational, and shows no evidence of restricted interests beyond typical childhood enthusiasms or social communication deficits.

encounter.referral_context: A 67-year-old male referred for cognitive assessment by the rehabilitation medicine team 3 weeks post-stroke (right middle cerebral artery CVA). Team is requesting evaluation to inform discharge planning and determine cognitive capacity for independent living.

But up here? I'm sharp as ever. I ran my own plumbing business for thirty years. I don't need anyone telling me I can't go home.

I was healthy as a horse until this thing hit me out of nowhere. I want my life back.

phases.clinician_prompt: Tell me about how things were for you before the stroke — your daily life, your routines.

He also said the patient tried to eat only the food on the right side of his plate until a nurse rotated it. The brother is worried but says the patient insists everything is fine.

questions.prompt: Based on the assessment findings, this patient's cognitive profile is most consistent with which DSM-5-TR neurocognitive diagnosis and its associated features?

options.A: Major Neurocognitive Disorder due to Alzheimer's Disease, with behavioral disturbance
options.B: Major Neurocognitive Disorder due to Cerebrovascular Disease, with prominent visuospatial and perceptual deficits
options.C: Mild Neurocognitive Disorder due to Cerebrovascular Disease, with prominent language deficits
options.D: Major Neurocognitive Disorder due to Cerebrovascular Disease, with prominent executive dysfunction

questions.explanation: The patient demonstrates major neurocognitive disorder due to a documented vascular event (right MCA stroke) with prominent visuospatial and perceptual deficits, specifically left hemispatial neglect with anosognosia. The deficits are severe enough to interfere with independence (cannot safely navigate environment, misses left-side stimuli), meeting criteria for 'major' rather than 'mild' severity. Verbal cognitive functions are preserved, consistent with a right-hemisphere lesion profile. The DSM-5-TR specifies that the perceptual-motor domain includes visuospatial and visuoconstructional abilities, which are the primary areas of impairment here.

distractor_rationale.A: Alzheimer's disease typically presents with prominent memory impairment as an early feature and follows a gradual progressive course. This patient's deficits have a clear acute vascular onset with a lateralized deficit pattern (left neglect) inconsistent with Alzheimer's presentation.
distractor_rationale.C: This patient demonstrates intact language functions — strong vocabulary, articulate speech, no word-finding difficulties. The deficits are visuospatial/perceptual, not linguistic. Additionally, 'mild' underestimates the severity given the functional impact on independence.
distractor_rationale.D: While executive dysfunction can accompany vascular neurocognitive disorder, the most prominent and functionally significant deficits in this case are visuospatial (left hemispatial neglect), not executive. The patient demonstrates adequate narrative coherence, goal-directed conversation, and good verbal reasoning, suggesting executive functions are relatively preserved.

questions.prompt: Given this patient's cognitive profile, anosognosia, and living situation, which recommendation is most appropriate for the discharge planning team?

options.A: Discharge home with outpatient speech-language therapy and weekly check-in calls from his brother
options.B: Discharge home with a home health aide, continued OT focusing on left-side scanning strategies, and a structured environmental safety assessment
options.C: Recommend long-term skilled nursing facility placement given the severity of cognitive deficits
options.D: Discharge home independently since verbal cognitive abilities are intact and the patient is motivated

questions.explanation: Given the significant left hemispatial neglect with anosognosia, this patient cannot safely live independently at this time — he is unaware of left-sided environmental hazards and resistant to compensatory strategies. However, the deficits are focal (visuospatial) with preserved verbal and general cognitive functioning, meaning there is rehabilitation potential. The most appropriate recommendation is a supported discharge with a home health aide for safety, continued occupational therapy to develop compensatory scanning strategies, and an environmental safety assessment of his two-story home to mitigate fall risk and left-side hazards.

distractor_rationale.A: Speech-language therapy is not indicated because the patient has no language deficits. Weekly check-in calls from a brother two hours away are insufficient safety support for a patient with hemispatial neglect and anosognosia who lives alone in a two-story home.
distractor_rationale.C: Long-term skilled nursing placement is premature. The patient has focal visuospatial deficits with otherwise intact cognition and strong premorbid functioning. He is only 3 weeks post-stroke with significant rehabilitation potential. A stepdown supportive approach is more appropriate and less restrictive.
distractor_rationale.D: Discharging independently is unsafe. Despite intact verbal abilities and high motivation, the patient has documented anosognosia for his left hemispatial neglect. He is unaware of left-sided hazards, as demonstrated by bumping into objects and missing left-side food. Motivation alone does not compensate for lack of deficit awareness, which is itself a neurological impairment.

questions.prompt: Based on the integrated assessment data — clinical interview, behavioral observations, collateral report, and MMPI-3 profile — which diagnosis is best supported as the primary diagnosis for this client?

options.A: Antisocial Personality Disorder
options.B: Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
options.C: Intermittent Explosive Disorder
options.D: Borderline Personality Disorder

questions.explanation: The integrated data most strongly support PTSD. The client has a well-documented history of severe childhood trauma (witnessing and experiencing domestic violence), recurrent trauma-related nightmares, hyperarousal symptoms (nocturnal thrashing, hyperstartle), dissociative-like reactivity to perceived threats, and emotional avoidance. The MMPI-3 profile shows elevated negative emotionality and dysfunctional negative emotions (RC7) alongside the antisocial behavior and aggression scales, which is consistent with a trauma-driven presentation rather than a primary characterological pattern.

distractor_rationale.A: While RC4 and AGG are elevated and there is a history of assaultive behavior, the full clinical picture suggests these behaviors are reactive and trauma-driven rather than reflecting the pervasive pattern of disregard for others' rights, deceitfulness, and lack of remorse characteristic of ASPD. The client expresses genuine remorse and his aggression appears linked to perceived threats.
distractor_rationale.C: Intermittent Explosive Disorder involves recurrent impulsive aggressive outbursts disproportionate to provocation, but it does not account for the nightmares, hyperarousal, emotional numbing, and trauma-related symptoms reported by collateral and observed during testing. IED also requires ruling out other conditions that better explain the aggression.
distractor_rationale.D: While emotional dysregulation and interpersonal intensity are present, the client does not demonstrate the identity disturbance, abandonment fears, self-harm, or chronic emptiness characteristic of BPD. The clinical picture is more consistent with trauma-related hyperarousal and threat reactivity.

questions.prompt: Given the MMPI-3 validity scale results showing moderately elevated L-r (68T) and mildly elevated K-r (62T) with F-r within normal limits, how should the clinician best interpret the overall validity of this protocol?

options.A: The protocol is invalid and should not be interpreted due to the elevated L-r and K-r scores
options.B: The elevations likely reflect deliberate malingering given the forensic context
options.C: The protocol is interpretable but the clinician should account for a tendency toward positive self-presentation that may attenuate clinical scale elevations
options.D: The validity scales suggest a random or inconsistent response pattern requiring readministration

questions.explanation: Moderate elevations on L-r and K-r with F-r within normal limits suggest a positive impression management style rather than protocol invalidity. In a forensic context, some degree of self-favorable responding is expected. The protocol remains interpretable, but the clinician should consider that clinical scale elevations may be somewhat attenuated (i.e., the true clinical picture may be more severe than what the substantive scales reflect).

distractor_rationale.A: Moderate elevations on L-r and K-r do not render a protocol invalid. MMPI-3 validity research indicates that profiles remain interpretable at these levels, though positive self-presentation should be factored into the interpretation. Invalidity is typically indicated by more extreme elevations or inconsistency indicators.
distractor_rationale.B: Malingering involves exaggeration or fabrication of symptoms, which would be reflected in elevated F-r or other overreporting indicators. The pattern here (elevated L-r and K-r with normal F-r) reflects underreporting or positive self-presentation, which is the opposite of malingering.
distractor_rationale.D: Random or inconsistent responding would be indicated by elevated TRIN-r and VRIN-r scores. The case notes indicate these are within acceptable ranges, ruling out inconsistent responding as a concern.

questions.prompt: Integrating the clinical interview, behavioral observations, Conners CPT-3 results, and multi-informant BASC-3 data, which primary diagnosis is best supported for D.M.?

options.A: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder, Predominantly Inattentive Presentation
options.B: Oppositional Defiant Disorder
options.C: Major Depressive Disorder
options.D: Disruptive Mood Dysregulation Disorder

questions.explanation: The integrated assessment data best support Major Depressive Disorder. The behavioral changes coincide with a clear psychosocial precipitant (parental separation), the highest elevations across informants are on Depression scales (T=74–76), the child endorses sadness, self-blame, social withdrawal, somatic complaints, and irritability. The aggression is reactive and accompanied by remorse, which is more consistent with an underlying mood disturbance manifesting as irritability than a primary externalizing disorder. The CPT-3 shows mild attention difficulties that are more consistent with concentration problems secondary to depression than primary ADHD.

distractor_rationale.A: While the CPT-3 shows mildly elevated omissions and variability, these scores are not in the clinically significant range for an ADHD diagnosis. The BASC-3 Attention Problems and Hyperactivity scores from both parent and teacher are not clinically elevated. Concentration difficulties secondary to depression can produce similar mild attention test findings, and the acute onset tied to psychosocial stressors argues against a neurodevelopmental condition.
distractor_rationale.B: Although D.M. displays argumentative, defiant, and aggressive behaviors, ODD requires a pattern of angry/irritable mood, argumentative/defiant behavior, or vindictiveness not better explained by another disorder. The reactive quality of the aggression, prominent sadness, self-blame, remorse after incidents, and the clear temporal connection to parental separation suggest these behaviors are better accounted for by a depressive disorder with irritable mood.
distractor_rationale.D: DMDD requires severe recurrent temper outbursts with persistently irritable or angry mood between outbursts, present for at least 12 months and observable in at least two settings. D.M.'s presentation has only been present for 6 months and his between-outburst presentation alternates between sadness/withdrawal and irritability rather than showing the persistently irritable baseline characteristic of DMDD. Additionally, onset must be before age 10 but symptoms must have been present for 12+ months.

questions.prompt: Given that D.M. expressed self-blame about his father's departure and said his father called him 'too much to deal with,' what should the clinician prioritize as the most important immediate clinical action?

options.D: Refer D.M. to a social skills group to address peer relationship difficulties

questions.explanation: Given D.M.'s depressive symptoms, self-blame cognitions, social isolation, and the intensity of his emotional distress, the most important immediate action is to conduct a developmentally appropriate suicide risk screening. Children as young as 8 can experience suicidal ideation, and D.M.'s profile of depression, perceived burdensomeness ('too much to deal with'), and social disconnection represent known risk factors. This does not mean the child is necessarily suicidal, but a responsible clinician must assess this before other interventions are planned.

distractor_rationale.A: While a psychiatric evaluation may ultimately be warranted, it is not the most immediate clinical priority during the current assessment session. Safety assessment must precede treatment recommendations. Additionally, the clinician has not yet completed the diagnostic formulation.
distractor_rationale.C: Family therapy may be an important component of treatment, but contacting the father is not the most urgent immediate clinical action. Furthermore, this requires careful planning and parental consent, and the immediate priority is ensuring the child's safety.
distractor_rationale.D: Addressing peer difficulties through a social skills group may be helpful as part of a longer-term treatment plan, but it does not address the immediate clinical concern of assessing safety in a child presenting with significant depressive symptoms and self-blame cognitions.

questions.prompt: Based on the integrated personality assessment data including PAI results, Rorschach (R-PAS) findings, clinical interview, behavioral observations, and collateral information, which diagnosis is most supported and should be the primary focus of treatment planning?

options.A: Bipolar II Disorder
options.B: Borderline Personality Disorder
options.C: Major Depressive Disorder, Recurrent, with Anxious Distress
options.D: Complex Posttraumatic Stress Disorder

questions.explanation: The integrated data converge on Borderline Personality Disorder as the primary diagnosis. The PAI BOR scale and subscales are all in the clinically significant range, with the highest elevations on Affective Instability and Self-Harm. The Rorschach findings show distorted human representations and elevated aggressive content consistent with disturbed interpersonal perception. Behavioral observations across the inpatient stay demonstrate classic splitting, and the clinical history reveals a chronic pattern of unstable relationships, identity disturbance, impulsivity, self-harm, chronic emptiness, and frantic efforts to avoid abandonment — meeting at least 6 of 9 DSM-5-TR BPD criteria.

distractor_rationale.A: The prior Bipolar II diagnosis is not supported by the current assessment. The PAI Mania scale is within normal limits (T=52), there are no documented manic or hypomanic episodes in outpatient records, and the mood instability observed is rapid and reactive to interpersonal events — characteristic of BPD affective instability rather than the episodic, sustained mood shifts of Bipolar II Disorder.
distractor_rationale.C: While depressive symptoms are present (PAI DEP: T=70), the depressive symptoms appear secondary to the pervasive personality pathology and are consistently precipitated by interpersonal disruptions rather than occurring independently. The chronic emptiness, self-harm, and affective instability are better accounted for by BPD than by a primary depressive disorder.
distractor_rationale.D: Although the patient reports childhood emotional neglect, she denies physical or sexual abuse, and the clinical presentation is more consistent with the DSM-5-TR BPD criteria than with a trauma-specific disorder. Complex PTSD (as conceptualized in ICD-11) shares features with BPD, but it is not a recognized DSM-5-TR diagnosis, and the assessment data more strongly point to characterological pathology with identity disturbance, splitting, and relational instability as the core features.

questions.prompt: The treatment team notes that a prior outpatient provider diagnosed this patient with Bipolar II Disorder and prescribed a mood stabilizer. When communicating your assessment findings to the treatment team, which consideration is most important regarding the diagnostic shift from Bipolar II to Borderline Personality Disorder?

options.A: BPD and Bipolar II cannot be comorbid, so the Bipolar II diagnosis must be formally ruled out before documenting BPD
options.B: The clinician should recommend discontinuing all mood stabilizing medication immediately since the Bipolar II diagnosis is not supported
options.C: The clinician should acknowledge the diagnostic complexity, present the multimethod assessment evidence, and discuss how affective instability in BPD can be misdiagnosed as bipolar spectrum disorder — particularly in women — while recommending integrated treatment planning
options.D: The clinician should defer to the prior provider's diagnosis since they had a longer treatment relationship with the patient

questions.explanation: The most appropriate approach is to acknowledge the diagnostic complexity, present the converging multimethod assessment data, and educate the team about how BPD affective instability is frequently misdiagnosed as bipolar disorder — a pattern that disproportionately affects women. Research consistently shows that BPD is underdiagnosed and bipolar disorder overdiagnosed in women with affective instability. The clinician should frame this as a refinement of understanding that can improve treatment planning (e.g., emphasizing evidence-based psychotherapy such as DBT rather than relying solely on pharmacotherapy).

distractor_rationale.A: BPD and Bipolar II can be comorbid — they are not mutually exclusive. However, the assessment data in this case do not support a comorbid Bipolar II diagnosis. The key clinical task is accurate differential diagnosis, not assuming mutual exclusivity.
distractor_rationale.B: Medication management decisions are outside the scope of the psychologist's assessment role, and recommending immediate discontinuation without psychiatric consultation could be clinically dangerous. The psychologist should share diagnostic findings with the prescribing provider and allow the physician to make medication decisions.
distractor_rationale.D: Length of treatment relationship does not determine diagnostic accuracy. The prior provider did not conduct formal psychological testing, and the current multimethod assessment provides a more rigorous evidence base for diagnostic formulation. A comprehensive personality assessment can meaningfully revise earlier clinical impressions.

questions.prompt: Given the referral question and presenting concerns, which assessment battery component is MOST essential to include in this neuropsychological evaluation?

options.A: A comprehensive measure of memory with immediate and delayed recall trials, such as the CVLT-3
options.B: A projective personality measure such as the Rorschach Performance Assessment System
options.C: An adaptive behavior rating scale such as the Vineland-3
options.D: A structured diagnostic interview for DSM-5 anxiety disorders

questions.explanation: The primary referral question concerns progressive memory decline. A comprehensive list-learning and memory measure such as the CVLT-3 is essential for characterizing the encoding, retrieval, and consolidation profile, which is critical in differentiating neurodegenerative memory impairment from other etiologies.

distractor_rationale.B: Projective personality measures are not indicated when the primary referral question is cognitive decline and there is no evidence of personality pathology or complex psychiatric differential.
distractor_rationale.C: The Vineland-3 is an adaptive behavior measure primarily used in intellectual disability and developmental evaluations, not standard in geriatric cognitive decline assessments.
distractor_rationale.D: A structured anxiety disorder interview is not warranted given the patient denies significant anxiety symptoms and the referral is for memory evaluation, not psychiatric differential diagnosis.

questions.prompt: Before interpreting neuropsychological test results for this patient, which cultural and demographic factor is MOST important to consider in ensuring valid interpretation?

options.A: The patient's premorbid occupational demands and educational attainment should inform selection of appropriate normative comparison groups
options.B: Neuropsychological tests are culture-free and require no demographic adjustments
options.C: Only age-corrected norms should be used; education and occupation are irrelevant to cognitive test performance
options.D: The patient's retirement status means premorbid cognitive estimates are unreliable and should not be calculated

questions.explanation: Neuropsychological test interpretation must account for demographic variables including age, education, and occupational history, which serve as proxies for premorbid functioning. Demographically corrected norms and premorbid estimates (e.g., based on reading recognition tasks) help distinguish true decline from baseline performance levels, reducing risk of both over- and under-diagnosis.

distractor_rationale.B: No neuropsychological test is truly culture-free. Cultural background, language, education, and socioeconomic status all affect test performance and must be considered during interpretation.
distractor_rationale.C: Education is one of the strongest predictors of neuropsychological test performance. Ignoring it would lead to inaccurate interpretation and potential misdiagnosis.
distractor_rationale.D: Retirement does not invalidate premorbid cognitive estimates. Occupational history, educational attainment, and reading-based measures remain valid proxies for estimating prior functioning.

Tonight we had a big blowup and she said she wants a separation. I went to the garage. I did have the gun out. I don't know what I was thinking exactly. I wasn't going to do anything. But… I don't know. Things just feel pretty dark right now. I haven't slept right in weeks.

phases.clinician_prompt: Mrs. T., can you tell me what you observed tonight and whether anything like this has happened before?

questions.prompt: Based on the full clinical picture, which combination of factors MOST significantly elevates this patient's acute suicide risk?

options.A: Chronic insomnia, job loss, and absence of prior psychiatric treatment
options.B: Acute alcohol intoxication, access to firearms, prior suicidal ideation, and acute psychosocial crisis
options.C: Marital conflict, male gender, and constricted affect during interview
options.D: Passive rather than active suicidal ideation, and identification of his daughter as a protective factor

questions.explanation: The convergence of acute alcohol intoxication (disinhibition), immediate access to lethal means (firearms), a history of prior suicidal ideation (driving into bridge), and an acute psychosocial crisis (job loss, marital separation) represents a high-risk constellation. Research consistently identifies access to firearms and acute intoxication as among the strongest proximal risk factors for completed suicide.

distractor_rationale.A: While insomnia, job loss, and lack of treatment are relevant risk factors, this combination omits the most critical proximal risk factors — intoxication, means access, and prior ideation — which together most elevate acute risk.
distractor_rationale.C: Male gender and marital conflict are distal/static risk factors, and constricted affect alone is a clinical observation, not a proximal risk factor. This combination does not capture the most dangerous acute elements.
distractor_rationale.D: This option describes mitigating/protective factors rather than risk-elevating factors. While the patient's ambivalence and protective connection to his daughter are clinically relevant, they do not represent the greatest risk elevation.

questions.prompt: Given this patient's current presentation and risk profile, what is the MOST appropriate immediate clinical recommendation?

options.A: Discharge with outpatient therapy referral and a safety plan, given that the patient denies active suicidal intent
options.B: Discharge once sober with instructions to lock firearms and follow up with his primary care physician within one week
options.C: Voluntary or involuntary psychiatric admission for stabilization, with lethal means counseling and coordination for firearm removal from the home
options.D: Administer a standardized depression inventory and defer disposition until results are scored

questions.explanation: Given the acute risk profile — intoxication, firearm access, prior ideation, acute crisis, and ambivalent suicidal thinking — psychiatric hospitalization for stabilization is the most appropriate intervention. Lethal means counseling and coordination to remove firearms from the home is a critical, evidence-based suicide prevention strategy that should accompany any disposition plan.

distractor_rationale.A: Discharging this patient based solely on his denial of active intent would be clinically inappropriate given the high-risk constellation. Denial of intent in the context of intoxication and acute crisis does not reliably indicate safety.
distractor_rationale.B: Simply instructing the patient to lock firearms and follow up with a PCP grossly underestimates the acute risk level. Means restriction requires active coordination (e.g., transferring firearms to a third party), not just verbal instructions to an intoxicated patient.
distractor_rationale.D: While standardized measures can supplement clinical judgment, deferring all disposition decisions pending a depression scale is insufficient and delays critical safety interventions in an acute risk situation.

encounter.referral_context: Referral from rehabilitation medicine physician for neuropsychological evaluation 6 weeks post-moderate traumatic brain injury (TBI) sustained in a motor vehicle accident, to assess cognitive functioning and inform rehabilitation planning.

phases.dialogue: They told me I need to do these tests so they can figure out what's going on with my brain. Honestly, I just want to get back to work. I'm a software engineer, and right now I can't even look at a screen for more than twenty minutes without getting a horrible headache. I keep losing my train of thought in the middle of conversations. My boyfriend says I've been really irritable, but I don't see it. I just want to know if I'm going to be normal again.

chart_reveals.label: Presenting Problem
chart_reveals.value: Cognitive complaints 6 weeks post-moderate TBI: difficulty sustaining attention, headaches with screen use, word-finding difficulty, irritability

chart_reveals.label: Injury Details
chart_reveals.value: MVA 6 weeks ago; GCS 10 at scene; LOC approximately 15 minutes; PTA estimated 3 days; brief ICU stay

phases.clinician_prompt: Can you tell me what's been most difficult for you since the accident?

phases.dialogue: Before the accident I was totally fine. Top of my team at work. I could hold like eight things in my head at once — that was kind of my superpower. Now I write something on a sticky note and forget where I put the sticky note. I'm sleeping a lot, like ten, eleven hours, and I still feel wiped. I've been crying more than usual, but I think that's just frustration. I'm not depressed. I was depressed in college — I know what that feels like — and this isn't that. This is like my brain is running on dial-up.

chart_reveals.label: Cognitive Complaints
chart_reveals.value: Reduced working memory, difficulty with multitasking, forgetfulness, word-finding difficulty — significant decline from high premorbid baseline

chart_reveals.label: Psychiatric History
chart_reveals.value: History of major depressive episode in college, treated with SSRI for ~1 year, no recurrence until present

chart_reveals.label: Somatic Symptoms
chart_reveals.value: Hypersomnia (10-11 hrs/night), persistent headaches exacerbated by cognitive exertion, photosensitivity

phases.clinician_prompt: How would you compare how your brain works now to how it worked before the accident?

phases.dialogue: Wait, sorry — can you repeat that? I was following and then it just… slipped away. I swear I'm trying. Is it hot in here? My head is really starting to pound. Can we take a break soon?

chart_reveals.label: Attention/Concentration
chart_reveals.value: Difficulty sustaining attention beyond 25-30 minutes; required multiple breaks; performance notably declined on tasks administered later in session

chart_reveals.label: Affect/Behavior
chart_reveals.value: Cooperative and motivated; became visibly frustrated and tearful during complex timed tasks; mood shifted from hopeful to defeated across session

chart_reveals.label: Effort/Validity
chart_reveals.value: All embedded and standalone performance validity tests within normal limits; effort judged adequate throughout

phases.dialogue: She's a completely different person. Before the accident she was so organized, so sharp. Now she'll start making dinner and walk away and forget about it. She snapped at her mom last weekend over nothing, and then she cried for an hour about it. She's trying so hard but she gets overwhelmed by the smallest things. I'm worried about her going back to work like this.

chart_reveals.label: Boyfriend Report
chart_reveals.value: Significant personality and functional change from premorbid baseline; emotional dysregulation, reduced executive functioning in daily tasks, easily overwhelmed

chart_reveals.label: Medical Records
chart_reveals.value: CT at admission: small right frontal contusion; follow-up MRI: resolved contusion with gliosis in right dorsolateral prefrontal region; no other structural abnormalities

chart_reveals.label: Premorbid Estimate
chart_reveals.value: WTAR standard score = 118; bachelor's degree in computer science from competitive university; employed as senior software engineer

phases.clinician_prompt: Can you describe the changes you've noticed in her since the accident?

questions.prompt: Based on the full assessment data — injury parameters, behavioral observations, collateral report, and neuroimaging — which diagnosis BEST accounts for this patient's current clinical presentation?

options.A: Major Depressive Disorder, recurrent episode
options.B: Neurocognitive Disorder Due to Traumatic Brain Injury, moderate severity
options.C: Somatic Symptom Disorder with predominant pain
options.D: Malingering

questions.explanation: The patient sustained a documented moderate TBI (GCS 10, LOC ~15 min, PTA ~3 days) with neuroimaging showing right frontal contusion and residual gliosis. Her cognitive complaints — reduced working memory, attentional difficulties, executive dysfunction — are consistent with the frontal injury location. Collateral report confirms marked functional decline from a high premorbid baseline. Performance validity testing was within normal limits, supporting genuine cognitive impairment rather than exaggeration.

distractor_rationale.A: While the patient exhibits emotional lability and hypersomnia, she explicitly differentiates her current experience from her prior depressive episode. Her emotional symptoms are better accounted for by neurobehavioral sequelae of frontal TBI (emotional dysregulation, irritability) rather than a primary mood disorder. Comorbid depressive features could be noted, but the primary diagnosis is the neurocognitive disorder.
distractor_rationale.C: Somatic Symptom Disorder requires disproportionate thoughts, feelings, or behaviors related to somatic symptoms. This patient has documented structural brain injury with objective neuroimaging findings, making her cognitive and somatic complaints a direct consequence of the TBI rather than a somatoform condition.
distractor_rationale.D: All performance validity tests were within normal limits, and the patient demonstrated consistent effort and genuine frustration throughout testing. There is no evidence of intentional symptom fabrication or exaggeration, and she has no apparent external incentive structure described in this evaluation context.

questions.prompt: Given the right dorsolateral prefrontal injury location and the patient's reported difficulties, which domain-specific measure would be MOST critical to include in this neuropsychological battery?

options.A: Wisconsin Card Sorting Test (WCST) to assess cognitive flexibility and set-shifting
options.B: Boston Naming Test (BNT) to assess confrontation naming ability
options.C: Wechsler Memory Scale – Visual Reproduction subtest to assess visual memory
options.D: Finger Tapping Test to assess motor speed bilaterally

questions.explanation: The right dorsolateral prefrontal cortex is critically involved in executive functions including cognitive flexibility, set-shifting, working memory, and planning. The WCST directly assesses cognitive flexibility and is sensitive to prefrontal dysfunction. Given that the patient's primary complaints involve working memory, multitasking, and cognitive control — all executive functions — measures targeting this domain are most critical.

distractor_rationale.B: The Boston Naming Test assesses confrontation naming, which is primarily a left temporal-parietal language function. While the patient reports some word-finding difficulty, the primary lesion is right frontal, making executive function measures more directly relevant to the injury location.
distractor_rationale.C: While visual memory assessment is reasonable in a comprehensive battery, the patient's primary complaints center on executive dysfunction (multitasking, working memory, cognitive control) rather than visual memory encoding. The injury location in the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex predicts executive rather than primary memory impairment.
distractor_rationale.D: The Finger Tapping Test assesses motor speed and would be relevant if there were motor complaints or suspected motor cortex involvement. The patient's primary deficits are cognitive and behavioral, and the lesion is in the dorsolateral prefrontal region, not primary motor cortex.

encounter.referral_context: Primary care physician referral for cognitive evaluation of a 67-year-old woman presenting with progressive memory complaints over the past 18 months. Husband reports she has been getting lost driving to familiar locations.

phases.dialogue: My doctor thought I should come in because I've been... you know, a little forgetful. Honestly, I think everyone my age forgets things. But my husband keeps making a fuss about it. I forgot how to get to my sister's house the other day — I've driven there a thousand times. That one did scare me a little, I'll admit.

chart_reveals.label: Presenting concern
chart_reveals.value: Progressive memory decline over 18 months; recent topographical disorientation

chart_reveals.label: Onset and course
chart_reveals.value: Gradual onset, progressive worsening; no acute precipitating event

phases.clinician_prompt: Can you tell me a bit about what brought you in today?

phases.dialogue: I retired about three years ago from teaching — third grade for thirty-two years. I loved it. Since retiring, I've been... well, I used to do crossword puzzles every morning and now they frustrate me so I stopped. I had a bout of depression maybe ten years ago when my mother passed, took medication for a while, but nothing like that now. I sleep fine, eat fine. I take blood pressure medication and something for cholesterol. No, I haven't had any falls or head injuries. My mother had... they called it senility back then. She was in a home by seventy-five.

chart_reveals.label: Functional decline
chart_reveals.value: Ceased cognitively demanding leisure activities (crossword puzzles); retired 3 years ago from teaching career

chart_reveals.label: Psychiatric history
chart_reveals.value: Single episode of major depression 10 years ago, treated with medication, resolved

chart_reveals.label: Family history
chart_reveals.value: Mother diagnosed with dementia ('senility'), placed in residential care at age 75

phases.clinician_prompt: Tell me a bit about your background, your health history, and whether anyone in your family has had memory problems.

phases.dialogue: Oh, those word lists are hard! I know I heard them, they're just... they're on the tip of my tongue. The shapes too — I drew them but I don't think they looked quite right. I got a bit flustered during the one where you kept reading numbers at me. I'm sorry, I feel like I'm not doing very well on any of this.

chart_reveals.label: Behavioral observations during testing
chart_reveals.value: Oriented to person and place; initially gave incorrect date (off by 3 days). Demonstrated significant effort on all tasks. Became visibly frustrated during memory and visuospatial tasks. Maintained appropriate social comportment throughout.

chart_reveals.label: Test engagement
chart_reveals.value: Performance validity indicators within acceptable limits; results considered valid representation of current functioning

phases.dialogue: She's changed. I don't know how else to say it. She was the sharpest person I ever met — managed our finances, organized everything. Now I find the car keys in the refrigerator. She asked me our grandson's name last week, and she's known him for twelve years. She still dresses herself fine and cooks okay, but she leaves the stove on. She repeats the same question three or four times in an hour without realizing it. It's not depression — I saw what that looked like. This is different.

chart_reveals.label: Husband's report
chart_reveals.value: Confirms progressive decline in memory and executive functioning; repetitive questioning; misplacing objects in unusual locations; forgetting well-known names. Preserved basic ADLs but emerging safety concerns (leaving stove on).

chart_reveals.label: Medical records
chart_reveals.value: Recent labs: TSH, B12, CBC, CMP all within normal limits. MRI brain (3 months ago): mild generalized cortical atrophy, bilateral hippocampal volume loss disproportionate to age. No evidence of cerebrovascular disease.

phases.clinician_prompt: Can you describe the changes you've noticed in your wife over the past year or so?

questions.prompt: Given this patient's presentation and referral question, which of the following test batteries would be most appropriate for the core assessment?

options.A: MMPI-3, PAI, and BDI-2 to rule out psychiatric contributors to cognitive decline
options.B: WAIS-IV, WMS-IV, Trail Making Test, Boston Naming Test, Rey Complex Figure, and Category Fluency
options.C: WISC-V, NEPSY-II, and Conners CPT-3 to assess attention and processing speed
options.D: Rorschach Performance Assessment System, TAT, and sentence completion to assess personality dynamics

questions.explanation: This patient presents with progressive memory decline, word-finding difficulties, visuospatial concerns, and executive dysfunction in the context of a positive family history and neuroimaging consistent with neurodegenerative disease. A comprehensive neuropsychological battery assessing intellectual functioning (WAIS-IV), memory (WMS-IV), executive functioning (Trail Making Test), language (Boston Naming Test, Category Fluency), and visuospatial abilities (Rey Complex Figure) is the standard of care for this referral question.

distractor_rationale.A: While ruling out psychiatric contributors is important, this battery focuses exclusively on personality and mood assessment and would not address the core referral question of characterizing cognitive decline. A mood screener could supplement the neuropsychological battery, but it should not be the primary assessment.
distractor_rationale.C: The WISC-V and NEPSY-II are pediatric instruments and entirely inappropriate for a 67-year-old adult. This option represents a fundamental test selection error.
distractor_rationale.D: Projective personality measures are not indicated for a cognitive evaluation referral. They would not provide data relevant to characterizing the nature and extent of cognitive decline or inform a neurodegenerative diagnostic formulation.

questions.prompt: Integrating all available data — clinical presentation, behavioral observations, collateral report, and neuroimaging — which diagnosis is best supported?

options.A: Major Depressive Disorder, recurrent episode, with cognitive features
options.B: Major Neurocognitive Disorder due to Alzheimer's Disease, probable
options.C: Mild Neurocognitive Disorder due to Alzheimer's Disease, probable
options.D: Major Neurocognitive Disorder due to Vascular Disease

questions.explanation: The patient demonstrates progressive decline in multiple cognitive domains (memory, language, visuospatial, executive functioning) with emerging functional impairment (safety concerns, cessation of complex activities). Collateral report confirms significant decline from baseline. MRI shows hippocampal atrophy without cerebrovascular disease, and medical causes have been ruled out. The insidious onset, progressive course, prominent amnestic presentation, family history, and neuroimaging pattern are consistent with Major Neurocognitive Disorder due to Alzheimer's Disease, probable.

distractor_rationale.A: The patient's prior depressive episode resolved and she currently denies depressive symptoms. Her husband specifically distinguishes the current presentation from her prior depression. While pseudodementia is an important differential, the progressive 18-month course, neuroimaging findings, and multi-domain cognitive decline argue against depression as the primary explanation.
distractor_rationale.C: Mild Neurocognitive Disorder would involve modest cognitive decline that does not interfere with independence in everyday activities. This patient's presentation — forgetting a grandson's name, getting lost to familiar locations, leaving the stove on, repetitive questioning — represents functional impairment beyond what is expected in the mild stage.
distractor_rationale.D: The MRI explicitly shows no evidence of cerebrovascular disease, making vascular etiology unlikely. The insidious onset and gradually progressive course are also more consistent with Alzheimer's disease than the stepwise decline typically seen in vascular neurocognitive disorder.

encounter.referral_context: Court-mandated psychological evaluation of a 34-year-old Vietnamese American man following a domestic disturbance call. His wife reported he was 'talking to spirits' and acting erratically. The court is requesting diagnostic clarification and treatment recommendations.

phases.dialogue: I don't know why I'm here. My wife called the police because she was scared, but she doesn't understand what was happening. I was praying. In my family, we communicate with our ancestors — it's something we've always done. My grandmother taught me. I wasn't hurting anyone. I was asking my grandfather's spirit for guidance because things have been very difficult. I know Americans think this is crazy, but it's not.

chart_reveals.label: Referral question
chart_reveals.value: Court-mandated diagnostic clarification; wife reported patient 'talking to spirits' and acting erratically

chart_reveals.label: Patient's account
chart_reveals.value: Describes incident as ancestral prayer/communication ritual taught by grandmother; denies any intent to harm self or others

phases.clinician_prompt: I appreciate you coming in today. Can you help me understand what happened the night the police were called?

phases.dialogue: My parents came here from Vietnam before I was born. My grandmother came later. She was a healer in our village — she could speak with ancestors and help people. She taught me some of these practices before she passed. I haven't done them in years, honestly. But three months ago, I lost my job — the company closed. We have two children. I couldn't sleep, couldn't eat. I felt like a failure. My wife works, but it's not enough. The shame of it... in my family, a man provides. My father would be so disappointed. That's when I started praying to my grandfather again, asking for help. But it got more intense. I was staying up all night doing it. My wife said I was talking louder and louder, and the kids got frightened. I... I can see how it looked. But I also haven't been myself. I feel empty. Like there's nothing left inside me.

chart_reveals.label: Precipitating stressors
chart_reveals.value: Job loss 3 months ago; significant financial strain; insomnia; appetite loss; feelings of emptiness and shame

chart_reveals.label: Cultural background
chart_reveals.value: Vietnamese American, second generation; grandmother was a traditional healer; ancestor communication is a recognized practice in family's cultural tradition

chart_reveals.label: Behavioral escalation
chart_reveals.value: Ancestral prayer practices intensified over past 6 weeks; staying up through the night; increasingly loud vocalizations during rituals; wife and children became frightened

phases.clinician_prompt: Can you tell me more about your background and what's been going on in your life leading up to that night?

phases.dialogue: I'm sorry, what was the question? I keep losing track. I'm very tired. I haven't slept more than a few hours in weeks. The tests with the questions and the pictures — I tried, but my mind just goes blank. I used to be sharp. I managed a warehouse, kept track of everything. Now I can't remember what you asked me five minutes ago. I don't hear voices if that's what you're wondering. When I pray to my grandfather, I feel his presence, but I don't hear him talk to me like a person in the room. It's more like... a feeling. A warmth. That's normal in our tradition.

chart_reveals.label: Observations
chart_reveals.value: Flat affect; psychomotor retardation; impaired concentration with frequent need for repetition; oriented x4. Denied auditory/visual hallucinations; described ancestral spiritual experiences as non-hallucinatory sensing of presence consistent with cultural practice. Speech slow, low volume, coherent. Thought process linear but sluggish. No loosening of associations or tangentiality.

chart_reveals.label: Test engagement
chart_reveals.value: Effortful but impaired sustained attention; required multiple breaks; appeared genuinely fatigued rather than unmotivated. Performance validity measures within acceptable limits.

phases.dialogue: He's not crazy. I want you to know that. I called the police because I was scared — he was up for three days straight, barely eating, talking loudly in the dark. But I grew up in a Vietnamese family too. I know what ancestor prayer looks like. This wasn't normal for him. It's like he couldn't stop. He used to be so steady, so calm. Since he lost his job, he's been a different person. He doesn't play with the kids anymore. He just sits and stares. Sometimes I find him crying in the garage at three in the morning. He told me once he thought we'd be better off without him. That's when I really got scared.

chart_reveals.label: Wife's report
chart_reveals.value: Confirms significant behavioral and personality change since job loss 3 months ago. Describes anhedonia, social withdrawal, tearfulness, insomnia, and passive suicidal ideation ('better off without him'). Notes that ancestral prayer practice is culturally normative but intensity and context are atypical for patient.

chart_reveals.label: Cultural context from informant
chart_reveals.value: Wife is also Vietnamese American and confirms ancestor communication is a recognized cultural and spiritual practice. States patient's recent behavior was atypical in its intensity and duration, not in its cultural basis.

phases.clinician_prompt: Can you tell me what you've observed in your husband over the past few months?

questions.prompt: Integrating all assessment data including cultural context, which diagnosis is most appropriate for this patient?

options.A: Schizophreniform Disorder
options.B: Brief Psychotic Disorder
options.C: Major Depressive Disorder, single episode, severe
options.D: Unspecified Schizophrenia Spectrum and Other Psychotic Disorder

questions.explanation: The patient presents with a clear depressive syndrome — depressed mood, anhedonia, insomnia, appetite loss, psychomotor retardation, concentration impairment, feelings of worthlessness/shame, and passive suicidal ideation — all precipitated by a significant stressor (job loss). Critically, his ancestral prayer practice is a culturally normative spiritual activity confirmed by both the patient and his wife, who shares the same cultural background. The intensification of the practice appears to be a culturally syntonic coping response to severe depression, not a psychotic phenomenon. He explicitly denies hallucinations and describes his spiritual experiences in terms consistent with his cultural tradition.

distractor_rationale.A: Schizophreniform Disorder requires psychotic symptoms such as delusions, hallucinations, or disorganized thinking. The patient's spiritual practices are culturally normative and not psychotic in nature. His thought process is linear and coherent, and he explicitly distinguishes his spiritual experiences from hallucinations. Misdiagnosing culturally sanctioned practices as psychosis is a well-documented assessment error.
distractor_rationale.B: Brief Psychotic Disorder similarly requires the presence of psychotic symptoms. There is no evidence of delusions, hallucinations, or disorganized speech/behavior when cultural context is appropriately considered. The behavioral escalation (staying up, loud vocalizations) is better explained by the severity of his depressive episode and sleep deprivation.
distractor_rationale.D: An unspecified psychotic disorder diagnosis would still require some evidence of psychotic phenomena. Applying a psychotic spectrum diagnosis in this case would represent a cultural misattribution — pathologizing a culturally normative spiritual practice. The DSM-5-TR explicitly cautions against diagnosing psychosis when experiences are consistent with cultural or religious norms.

questions.prompt: Based on the diagnostic formulation and cultural context of this case, which treatment approach is most appropriate to recommend to the court?

options.A: Antipsychotic medication and a structured psychoeducation program about reality testing
options.B: Immediate inpatient psychiatric hospitalization for psychotic disorder stabilization
options.C: Individual therapy integrating culturally adapted CBT for depression, safety planning for suicidal ideation, psychiatric consultation for antidepressant medication, and coordination with a culturally congruent support system
options.D: Referral for couple's therapy to address marital conflict as the primary treatment focus

questions.explanation: The appropriate treatment plan addresses the diagnosed Major Depressive Disorder while respecting the patient's cultural identity. Culturally adapted CBT for depression has strong empirical support and can integrate the patient's cultural values and coping practices. Given the passive suicidal ideation reported by his wife, safety planning is essential. Psychiatric consultation for antidepressant medication is warranted given symptom severity. Involving culturally congruent supports (family, community, potentially a traditional healer if the patient desires) reflects best practices in culturally responsive care.

distractor_rationale.A: Prescribing antipsychotics and reality-testing psychoeducation would be predicated on a psychotic diagnosis, which is not supported by the assessment data when cultural context is appropriately considered. This approach would pathologize the patient's cultural practices and likely damage therapeutic alliance.
distractor_rationale.B: There is no indication for inpatient psychiatric hospitalization. The patient is not acutely psychotic, and his suicidal ideation is passive. While safety must be monitored, involuntary hospitalization based on a misattribution of cultural practices as psychosis would be clinically inappropriate and potentially harmful.
distractor_rationale.D: While the couple's relationship has been strained, marital conflict is not the primary clinical concern. The patient is experiencing a severe depressive episode with suicidal ideation. Couple's therapy might be a useful adjunct later, but it does not address the urgent clinical needs of treating depression and ensuring safety.

encounter.setting: Pediatric psychology consultation service within a children's hospital
encounter.referral_context: Inpatient pediatric team requests psychological consultation for a 15-year-old girl admitted for recurrent syncope and non-epileptic seizure-like episodes. Neurology has ruled out epilepsy and cardiac causes. The team suspects a psychological component but the patient and her mother are resistant to the referral.

phases.dialogue: I don't need to talk to a psychologist. There's something wrong with my body and nobody can figure it out. I keep fainting and having these episodes where my whole body shakes and I can't control it. It's terrifying. Everyone keeps saying my tests are normal, like I'm making this up. I'm not making it up. You can ask my mom — she's seen them happen. They started about four months ago and they're getting worse. I had three last week.

chart_reveals.label: Presenting symptoms
chart_reveals.value: Recurrent syncope and seizure-like episodes (shaking, loss of responsiveness) x 4 months, increasing frequency

chart_reveals.label: Medical workup
chart_reveals.value: Normal EEG (including 48-hour video EEG with captured events showing no epileptiform activity); normal cardiac workup (echocardiogram, Holter monitor, tilt table test); normal brain MRI; basic labs WNL

phases.clinician_prompt: I'm Dr. [name]. The medical team asked me to come by because they want to make sure we're looking at the whole picture. Can you tell me what's been going on?

phases.dialogue: I was fine before all this started. I'm a sophomore, I do — I did — competitive gymnastics. I was being scouted for a club team. Then in September I had the first episode at practice. I just collapsed. After that, they kept happening. At school, at home, at practice. My coach benched me because she's worried about liability. I lost my spot on the competition team. That's the worst part — gymnastics is everything to me. I've done it since I was five. My whole plan was to compete in college. Now I can barely go to school because I never know when one will hit. Sometimes I feel lightheaded before, sometimes it just happens. When I shake, I can sort of hear people around me but I can't respond. It's like being trapped. No, I've never had any mental health treatment. I don't have anxiety or depression. I mean, I'm upset about missing gymnastics, obviously, but who wouldn't be?
